items.title: Potemkin Loader & RMMProject The Anatomy of a ClickFix Attack
items.description: A ClickFix social engineering attack on an unmonitored endpoint led to a multi-stage intrusion affecting over 11 hosts. The infection chain began with a malicious HTA payload that silently installed an MSI package containing Potemkin, a custom loader with a deterministic DGA. Potemkin delivered RMMProject, a 4.4 MB Lua-scriptable RAT featuring browser credential theft with Chrome App-Bound Encrypt
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix social engineering attack exploited an unmonitored endpoint, leading to a multi-stage intrusion affecting over 11 hosts. The infection chain began with a malicious HTA payload that silently installed an MSI package containing Potemkin, a custom loader with a deterministic DGA, which delivered RMMProject, a 4.4 MB Lua-scriptable RAT. This RAT featured browser credential theft with Chrome App-Bound Encryption, allowing the threat actors to steal sensitive information. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust endpoint monitoring, restrict user execution of unknown files, and utilize a web application firewall to filter out malicious HTA payloads. Additionally, defenders should prioritize patching any vulnerabilities related to remote access software and ensure that all software is up-to-date, specifically focusing on Chrome browser updates to prevent credential theft.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1555.003| E[Credentials from Web Browsers]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Attackers Weaponize Microsoft Teams Relays to Stay Hidden
items.description: Attackers deploying DragonForce ransomware against a major U.S. services firm concealed their command-and-control traffic within Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure using Backdoor.Turn, a custom Go-based remote access trojan. This novel technique leverages anonymous Teams visitor tokens and TURN relay servers to mask malicious communications as legitimate Microsoft traffic. The intrusion lasted o
items.ai_summary: The DragonForce ransomware attack against a major U.S. services firm exploited the Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure using a custom Go-based remote access trojan known as Backdoor.Turn. This novel technique leveraged anonymous Teams visitor tokens and TURN relay servers to mask malicious communications as legitimate Microsoft traffic, making it challenging for defenders to detect. The attack involved exploiting public-facing applications (T1190) and utilizing application layer protocols (T1071) to establish remote access (T1219) and ultimately encrypt data for impact (T1486). To remediate, defenders should implement strict traffic monitoring and filtering rules for Microsoft Teams traffic, ensuring that all communications are authenticated and authorized. Additionally, defenders should update their threat intelligence feeds to include indicators of compromise (IOCs) associated with Backdoor.Turn and DragonForce ransomware.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Bluekit Phishing as a Service (PhaaS)
items.description: BlueKit operates as a mature commercial Phishing-as-a-Service platform offering 87 ready-made phishing kits targeting banks, cloud services, cryptocurrency exchanges, and global brands. The platform features subscription-based access, automated account takeover capabilities, peer-to-peer infrastructure for stealth, and integrated anti-detection tooling. BlueKit supports credential harvesting, sess
items.ai_summary: The BlueKit Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) platform poses a significant threat to various organizations, including banks, cloud services, cryptocurrency exchanges, and global brands, by offering 87 ready-made phishing kits. These kits enable threat actors to compromise accounts through credential harvesting and session hijacking, leveraging tactics such as phishing (T1566) and utilizing valid accounts (T1078) to gain unauthorized access. The real-world impact of BlueKit's operations can result in financial loss, data breaches, and compromised user accounts, affecting both individuals and businesses. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering solutions, conduct regular security awareness training for employees, and enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all users. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious account activity and implementing a robust incident response plan can help mitigate the effects of a BlueKit phishing campaign.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1078| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1586| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1078| E[Persistence]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: New APT-Q-27 sample spotted
items.description: A new campaign has been identified utilizing a valid digital signature from a Chinese technology company that remains unrevoked. The attack chain employs a dropper that retrieves an extension-based module list from command and control infrastructure. The malicious payloads exploit DLL Side-Loading techniques through a legitimate Tencent-signed executable to achieve code execution. The infrastructu
items.ai_summary: The newly identified APT-Q-27 sample utilizes a valid digital signature from a Chinese technology company to evade detection, exploiting DLL Side-Loading techniques through a legitimate Tencent-signed executable to achieve code execution. This attack chain employs a dropper that retrieves an extension-based module list from command and control infrastructure, leveraging T1059 Command and Scripting Interpreter and T1574.002 DLL Side-Loading techniques. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access and data breaches, particularly affecting organizations with outdated or unpatched systems. To remediate, defenders should implement strict validation of digital signatures, monitor for suspicious activity related to the Tencent-signed executable, and ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, network traffic should be monitored for unusual application layer protocol activity, as indicated by T1071.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1574.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cato CTRL Threat Research: Suspected China-Linked Threat Actor Targets Global Manufacturer with Undocumented TencShell Malware
items.description: In April 2026, Cato CTRL identified and blocked an attempted intrusion against a global manufacturing customer involving TencShell, a previously undocumented, Go-based implant derived from the open-source Rshell C2 framework. The activity appeared in traffic associated with a third-party user connected to the customer environment.
items.ai_summary: The suspected China-linked threat actor utilized the undocumented TencShell malware, a Go-based implant derived from the open-source Rshell C2 framework, to target a global manufacturer. The attack vector involved exploiting public-facing applications, as indicated by the detection of TTP T1190, and leveraging application layer protocols, as seen in T1071. If successfully exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access and potential data breaches, affecting the confidentiality and integrity of the manufacturer's data. To remediate, defenders should implement robust network traffic monitoring, focusing on unusual patterns and connections to unknown servers, and ensure all public-facing applications are configured with the latest security patches and secure communication protocols. Specifically, defenders should verify that their security controls can detect and block Go-based malware and unknown C2 frameworks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: 140+ npm Packages Compromised in Coordinated Supply Chain Attack
items.description: More than 140 Mastra npm packages were compromised through a supply chain attack that injected a typosquatted dependency called easy-day-js. A single npm account published malicious versions within a short timeframe, affecting packages including @mastra/core with over 918K weekly downloads. The attack executes during npm install via a postinstall hook, deploying a two-stage payload. The first stag
items.ai_summary: The recent supply chain attack on Mastra npm packages has compromised over 140 packages, including @mastra/core, which has over 918K weekly downloads. The attack was carried out by injecting a malicious dependency called easy-day-js, which is a typosquatted version of a legitimate package. The malicious package executes during npm install via a postinstall hook, deploying a two-stage payload, with the first stage likely used for reconnaissance and the second stage for deploying the actual malware. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately remove any affected packages and update their dependencies to versions that do not include the easy-day-js package, and also monitor their systems for any suspicious activity related to the TTPs detected, including T1583.001, T1608, and T1195. Additionally, developers should verify the integrity of their dependencies and use tools like npm audit to identify and fix any vulnerabilities in their projects.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583.001| C[Collection]:::tactic
    C -->|T1608| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Execution]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From emerging threat to top-tier ransomware-as-a-service: The evolution of INC ransomware
items.description: INC has evolved from an emerging ransomware-as-a-service operation into one of the most active groups in 2026, claiming over 800 victims since 2023. The disruption of LockBit and BlackCat's shutdown created opportunities for INC to expand as affiliates migrated. Both Windows and Linux/ESXi encryptors have been rewritten in Rust, enabling cross-platform development and increasing analysis complexit
items.ai_summary: The INC ransomware-as-a-service operation has evolved significantly, claiming over 800 victims since 2023, and has become one of the most active groups in 2026. The group's Windows and Linux/ESXi encryptors have been rewritten in Rust, allowing for cross-platform development and increased analysis complexity. If exploited, victims can expect their data to be encrypted, leading to significant disruptions and potential financial losses. To remediate, defenders should ensure all systems are up-to-date, implement a robust backup strategy, and consider implementing a security information and event management (SIEM) system to detect and respond to potential incidents. Additionally, defenders should prioritize network segmentation, implement least privilege access, and regularly conduct vulnerability assessments to reduce the attack surface.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1529| C[System Shutdown/Reboot]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: More Than 4,000 Legacy Routers Compromised by AryStinger, Turned into Global Attack Proxies for Hackers
items.description: Security researchers discovered AryStinger, a botnet targeting legacy routers and NAS devices to build reconnaissance and attack infrastructure. The malware exploits vulnerabilities from 2013-2025 to compromise over 4,300 devices globally, primarily D-Link routers using RTL819X chips. AryStinger communicates via HTTP/HTTPS using Protobuf encoding and XOR encryption, supporting tasks including netw
items.ai_summary: The AryStinger botnet has compromised over 4,300 legacy routers and NAS devices worldwide, primarily targeting D-Link routers with RTL819X chips. The malware exploits vulnerabilities from 2013-2025, allowing attackers to build a global attack proxy infrastructure. If exploited, affected devices can be used for malicious activities such as reconnaissance and attacks on other networks. To remediate, defenders should immediately update their router firmware to the latest version, change default passwords, and disable remote management interfaces. Additionally, network administrators should monitor their devices for suspicious HTTP/HTTPS traffic using Protobuf encoding and XOR encryption, and consider implementing a network segmentation strategy to limit the spread of malware.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583| B[Acquire Infrastructure]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploitation for Client Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1566| E[Initial Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix Campaign Generated Via AI Delivers SmartRAT
items.description: In March 2026, threat actors leveraged AI-powered website builders to create typosquatting domains impersonating a Brazilian bank. The campaign employed ClickFix techniques, presenting victims with fake CAPTCHA and BSOD screens to trick them into executing malicious PowerShell commands. This delivered SmartRAT, a PowerShell-based banking trojan with capabilities including encrypted C2 communicatio
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign utilizes AI-powered website builders to create typosquatting domains, specifically targeting a Brazilian bank, to deliver SmartRAT, a PowerShell-based banking trojan. This campaign employs social engineering tactics, presenting victims with fake CAPTCHA and BSOD screens, to trick them into executing malicious PowerShell commands, leveraging T1059.001. The SmartRAT trojan enables encrypted C2 communication, as indicated by T1573, allowing threat actors to maintain persistence and exfiltrate sensitive information. To remediate, defenders should implement PowerShell version 7 or later, with constrained language mode, and restrict execution of PowerShell scripts from untrusted sources. Additionally, monitoring for unusual network activity, specifically targeting T1071 application layer protocol anomalies, can help detect potential SmartRAT communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1573| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Copycat hits another npm package
items.description: A Shai-Hulud copycat worm has infected the npm package chalk-tempalte, appearing just five days after the original worm was open-sourced by its creators. The same threat actor also published three additional malicious npm packages containing infostealer code: @deadcode09284814/axios-util, axois-utils, and color-style-utils. These packages collectively received 2,678 weekly downloads and contain va
items.ai_summary: The Shai-Hulud copycat worm has infected the npm package chalk-tempalte, leveraging the same tactics as the original worm that was open-sourced by its creators just five days prior. This threat actor has also published three additional malicious npm packages, @deadcode09284814/axios-util, axois-utils, and color-style-utils, which contain infostealer code and have collectively received 2,678 weekly downloads. The attack vector involves exploiting public-facing applications (T1190) and compromising software dependencies (T1195.001), allowing the threat actor to potentially steal sensitive information from affected systems. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the infected packages and update their dependencies to the latest versions, ensuring that all packages are verified through npm audit and npm install with the --audit flag. Additionally, developers should implement robust dependency management and monitoring to detect similar threats in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195.001| C[Compromise Software Dependencies]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-43973: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption vulnerability in ninenines gun (gun_http modul...
items.description:
Uncontrolled Resource Consumption vulnerability in ninenines gun (gun_http module) allows a malicious server to exhaust client memory via unbounded HTTP/1.1 response buffering.

In gun_http:handle/5, three clauses accumulate incoming TCP data into the connection's buffer field using binary concatenation with no upper-bound check: the head clause appends data until the \r\n\r\n header terminator is
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-43973 is an Uncontrolled Resource Consumption vulnerability in the ninenines gun gun_http module, allowing a malicious server to exhaust client memory via unbounded HTTP/1.1 response buffering. The vulnerability is caused by the accumulation of incoming TCP data into the connection's buffer field using binary concatenation with no upper-bound check in the gun_http:handle/5 function. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to a denial-of-service (DoS) condition, affecting applications that rely on the gun_http module, such as web servers and APIs. To remediate this vulnerability, developers should update the gun_http module to a version that includes a fix for this issue, such as implementing a buffer size limit or using a more efficient data processing mechanism. Additionally, defenders can mitigate the impact of this vulnerability by implementing rate limiting and IP blocking on their web servers to prevent malicious traffic from exhausting client resources.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1490| C[Denial of Service]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1562| E[Data Manipulation]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-43972: Origin Validation Error vulnerability in ninenines gun (gun_http2 module) allows...
items.description:
Origin Validation Error vulnerability in ninenines gun (gun_http2 module) allows cross-origin cookie injection via unvalidated HTTP/2 PUSH_PROMISE authority.

In gun_http2:push_promise_frame/7, the :authority pseudo-header from an incoming PUSH_PROMISE frame is stored verbatim into the promised stream record without checking that it matches the connection's origin. When gun_http2:headers_frame/9 l
items.ai_summary: The CVE-2026-43972 vulnerability is an Origin Validation Error in the ninenines gun (gun_http2 module) that allows cross-origin cookie injection via unvalidated HTTP/2 PUSH_PROMISE authority. This occurs because the :authority pseudo-header from an incoming PUSH_PROMISE frame is stored without checking that it matches the connection's origin, potentially allowing an attacker to inject malicious cookies. The real-world impact of this vulnerability is that an attacker could exploit it to perform session fixation or cookie tampering attacks, potentially affecting users of applications that rely on the gun_http2 module. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update to a version of gun that includes the fix for CVE-2026-43972, and consider implementing additional security measures such as validating the :authority pseudo-header in incoming PUSH_PROMISE frames. Defenders should also monitor their applications for signs of cross-origin cookie injection and take swift action if such activity is detected.

    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-36789: Shenzhen Tenda Technology Co., Ltd Tenda AC1206 v15.03.06.23 was discovered to c...
items.description: Shenzhen Tenda Technology Co., Ltd Tenda AC1206 v15.03.06.23 was discovered to contain multiple stack overflows in the fromGstDhcpSetSer function via the username and password parameters. These vulnerabilities allow attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted HTTP request.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-36789 is a stack overflow vulnerability in the fromGstDhcpSetSer function of Shenzhen Tenda Technology Co., Ltd's Tenda AC1206 router, version v15.03.06.23. The vulnerability can be exploited via crafted HTTP requests, specifically by manipulating the username and password parameters, allowing attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS). If exploited, this vulnerability can render the router unavailable, affecting users who rely on it for internet access. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update their Tenda AC1206 router to a version later than v15.03.06.23, and ensure that their router's firmware is updated to the latest version. Additionally, users can mitigate the risk of exploitation by restricting access to the router's web interface and implementing network segmentation.

items.title: CVE-2026-25558: QloApps through 1.7.0 contains a stored cross-site scripting vulnerability in th...
items.description: QloApps through 1.7.0 contains a stored cross-site scripting vulnerability in the admin file manager that allows authenticated administrators to inject malicious JavaScript by uploading crafted SVG files. Attackers can embed JavaScript event handlers such as onload within SVG files uploaded through the file manager to execute arbitrary scripts in the browser of any user who subsequently views the
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-25558 is a stored cross-site scripting vulnerability in QloApps versions 1.7.0 and earlier, specifically within the admin file manager. This vulnerability allows authenticated administrators to inject malicious JavaScript by uploading crafted SVG files, which can then execute arbitrary scripts in the browser of any user who views the uploaded file. The attack vector involves embedding JavaScript event handlers, such as onload, within the SVG files. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update QloApps to version 1.7.1 or later and restrict file uploads to only necessary file types. Additionally, implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) with rules to detect and prevent XSS attacks can provide an extra layer of protection.
items.workflow_graph:

This affects an unknown part of the file src/main/java/com/alien/bank/management/system/controller/TransactionController.java of the component Transaction Endpoint. Such manipulation leads to improper authorization. It is possible to launch the attack remot
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11521 is a security vulnerability in the Mohammed-eid35 bank-management-system-springboot, specifically affecting the TransactionController.java file in the Transaction Endpoint component. This vulnerability allows for improper authorization, potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive financial information. The vulnerability can be exploited remotely, and it is recommended that users update to a version later than 7b9bcc65ad7df3db29af71aed9bb500e5f24d948 to mitigate the risk. In a real-world scenario, this vulnerability could be exploited by an attacker to gain unauthorized access to bank accounts, leading to financial loss and identity theft. To remediate, defenders should update their bank-management-system-springboot to the latest version and ensure that all dependencies are up-to-date, specifically focusing on the Transaction Endpoint component.

items.title: ShapedPlugin update flow hacked to infect WordPress sites
items.description: Multiple WordPress plugins from ShapedPlugin were compromised in a supply chain attack that distributed infected releases to paying customers via the vendor's official update system. [...]

items.title: FortiBleed leak exposes Fortinet VPN credentials for 73,000 devices.
items.description: A newly discovered data leak dubbed "FortiBleed" has exposed what appears to be a collection of Fortinet and FortiGate VPN credentials for 73,932 firewall URLs at organizations worldwide. [...]

items.title: No Exploits Required
items.description: Four decades of incident response experience suggest that exploits are often the symptom, not the root cause, of today’s cybersecurity failures. The post No Exploits Required appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Apple fixes Beats Studio Buds flaw that let hackers spy on conversations
items.description: Apple has released security updates to patch a high-severity flaw affecting the Beats Studio Buds wireless earbuds that could allow attackers in Bluetooth range to spy on users' conversations. [...]

items.title: Telegram admits it couldn't police exam-leak channels, India tells court
items.description: India's government has told the Delhi High Court that Telegram was warned about two weeks before it was blocked, and that the platform admitted it could not proactively detect the channels selling leaked exam papers. Telegram says it cooperated and the ban is unlawful. [...]

items.title: Orphaned AI Agents: How to Find Hidden Access Risks Inside Your Network
items.description: If an autonomous AI agent interacts with your company's core intellectual property today, can your security team instantly name the person who authorized it? For most enterprises, the answer is a simple no. The rush to adopt internal AI tools has left a massive trail of administrative debt: orphaned agents (AI tools left running after their creator leaves the company) and standing privileges (

items.title: Dream Raises $260 Million at $3 Billion Valuation
items.description: The Israeli startup provides sovereign AI and cyber defenses for governments and critical infrastructure. The post Dream Raises $260 Million at $3 Billion Valuation appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: F5 issues out-of-band patches for critical NGINX vulnerabilities
items.description: Cybersecurity company F5 has released out-of-band security updates to address multiple NGINX web server vulnerabilities, including two critical-severity flaws that could allow attackers to execute code on vulnerable systems. [...]

items.title: The Scripts on Your Checkout Page Are Now a PCI DSS Problem
items.description: An independent PCI assessor tested Reflectiz against the new PCI DSS rules. Here is the verdict: See the full QSA assessment here → When a customer types their card number into your checkout, their browser is running far more than your code. Analytics tags, a tag manager, a support widget, a payment iframe: a modern checkout loads dozens of third-party scripts, and any one of them can be turned

items.title: Atlassian, Splunk Patch Critical Vulnerabilities
items.description: Splunk patched an OS command injection in AI Toolkit, while Atlassian fixed dozens of flaws in third-party dependencies. The post Atlassian, Splunk Patch Critical Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Rokarolla Banking Trojan Targets 200 Applications
items.description: The Android malware allows its operators to take control of infected devices and harvest sensitive information. The post Rokarolla Banking Trojan Targets 200 Applications appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Critical Command Execution Vulnerability Patched in Cisco ISE
items.description: Insufficient validation of user input allows an attacker to gain access to the underlying OS and elevate their privileges to root. The post Critical Command Execution Vulnerability Patched in Cisco ISE appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft fixes Windows Server 2016 security update failures
items.description: Microsoft has fixed a known issue causing the June 2026 security updates to fail on Windows Server 2016 systems that weren't up to date. [...]

items.title: F5 Patches Critical, High-Severity NGINX Vulnerabilities
items.description: Critical flaws in NGINX could allow remote, unauthenticated attackers to cause a restart and potentially execute arbitrary code. The post F5 Patches Critical, High-Severity NGINX Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: SailPoint to Acquire Entro in Reported $200 Million Deal
items.description: Israel-based Entro specializes in non-human identity and credential security solutions, and it will enable SailPoint to enhance its products. The post SailPoint to Acquire Entro in Reported $200 Million Deal appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Kodak Admits Data Breach After ShinyHunters Hack Claims
items.description: Kodak told SecurityWeek it believes there is no threat to its systems or operations as a result of the cybersecurity incident. The post Kodak Admits Data Breach After ShinyHunters Hack Claims appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
items.description: "Shield-6G" will combine AI threat detection, digital twins, honeypots, and more, to help carriers protect 6G networks against the threats of tomorrow.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Leak confirms OpenAI is testing a ChatGPT for Science subscription
items.description: OpenAI appears to be testing a new subscription and experience for science use cases, but it's unclear if it'll be available to everyone regardless of their background. [...]

items.title: Google to use UK and EU user IP addresses for ad personalization
items.description: From August 3, 2026, Google will use IP addresses from UK, EEA and Switzerland users for ad measurement and personalization. It lands as the ICO weighs new consent rules, and years after Google itself called using such signals to identify devices "wrong." [...]

items.title: INC Ransomware Thrives by Mastering the Basics
items.description: And one of those basics is focusing on sectors where a ransomware disruption creates immediate pressure to pay up, like with healthcare.

items.title: Crypto Clipper Campaign Abuses Fake Reviews, AI Narrators, and VirusTotal Comments
items.description: An unknown threat actor has been observed leveraging paid or promoted posts on legitimate news websites to drum up buzz for their warez, according to new findings from Check Point Research. The threat actor also has at their disposal a dedicated WordPress phishing page that acts as the central hub, alongside GitHub and SourceForge projects promoted by fake accounts, a YouTube channel, and a

items.title: Hostile states behind three-quarters of attacks on Britain's critical infrastructure, cyber chief warns
items.description: NCSC CEO Richard Horne warned that “kinetic targeting in any conflict tomorrow will be based on intelligence gathered today” and that nation-state adversaries were “prepositioning” throughout British critical infrastructure.

items.title: Microsoft Confirms RoguePlanet Defender Zero-Day, Says Patch is in Development
items.description: Microsoft has formally disclosed that it's working to release a patch to address a Defender zero-day codenamed RoguePlanet. The vulnerability has now been assigned the CVE identifier CVE-2026-50656 (CVSS score: 7.8), with the tech giant describing it as a privilege escalation flaw. "Microsoft is aware of an elevation of privilege in the Microsoft Malware Protection Engine in Microsoft Defender

items.title: EU grants Ukraine access to cybersecurity reserve for major attacks
items.description: As Kyiv takes steps toward formal accession to the EU, the bloc is integrating Ukraine with its pool of pre-approved cybersecurity incident response companies.

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: Junior Hacker Used Tailscale and OpenSSH to Keep Access After His C2 Went Offline
items.description: A French-speaking attacker broke into a small French automotive business, planted a keylogger, and stole banking and email credentials. Ordinary stuff, until one move near the end. Before his command-and-control server went dark, he installed OpenSSH and Tailscale on a victim's machine, building a way back in that did not run through the C2 at all. When the Havoc server went offline the next

items.title: Adversarial Exposure Validation Turns Security Visibility into Confident Prioritization
items.description: For security teams, the findings never stop, but confidence in knowing which ones matter is becoming harder to maintain. The problem is no longer visibility. It's validation. Security teams must decide which findings warrant action while operating under constant pressure and incomplete information. Increasingly, the challenge is not discovering potential risks. It is determining which risks

items.title: Webinar Today: How Modern Breaches Bypass MFA and Evade Detection
items.description: Attendees will learn how attackers evade conventional detection methods, why legacy MFA alone is no longer sufficient, and how organizations can strengthen their defenses. The post Webinar Today: How Modern Breaches Bypass MFA and Evade Detection appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
items.description: Attackers are actively targeting various sectors across nearly 200 countries and already have compiled a list of working credentials for tens of thousands of compromised devices.

items.title: Malicious JetBrains Plugins Steal AI API Keys as Chrome Extensions Capture Chatbot Chats
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a "coordinated malware campaign" on the JetBrains Marketplace that has published no less than 15 malicious plugins capable of exfiltrating artificial intelligence (AI) provider keys. "Every plugin poses as an AI coding assistant built on DeepSeek and other large language models, offering chat, commit messages, code review, bug finding, and unit tests,"

items.title: The Top 10 Attack Surface Exposures in 2026
items.description: Breaches don't always start with a zero-day. An exposed admin panel can get brute-forced, or credentials reused from a previous attack. But when a vulnerability does drop — like MongoBleed earlier this year, which let attackers pull credentials and session tokens from server memory without authentication — anything internet-facing is immediately at risk. With time-to-exploit now down to a

items.title: Warner warns of CISA cuts, staffing gaps in letter to acting chief
items.description: Warner on Tuesday also wrote a letter to DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin, underscoring that DHS must prioritize CISA and pay for the MS-ISAC.

items.title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
items.description: The UK will ban adolescents under 16 years old from user-to-user social media platforms, despite age verification issues and privacy concerns.

items.title: 145 Mastra npm Packages Compromised via Hijacked Contributor Account
items.description: As many as 145 npm packages associated with the Mastra namespace ("@mastra/*"), a popular open-source JavaScript and TypeScript framework for building artificial intelligence (AI) applications, have been compromised as part of a software supply chain attack codenamed easy-day-js, per findings from Endor Labs, JFrog, OX Security, SafeDep, Socket, StepSecurity, and Synk. "A single npm account (

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 17th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9976, (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: India's Telegram ban draws criticism from Durov as company challenges order in court
items.description: To prevent cheating, Indian authorities ordered Telegram to restrict access nationwide ahead of a major medical entrance exam.

items.title: GitHub dismissed security reports on flaws now exploited by supply-chain worm, researchers say
items.description: GitHub rejected two formal vulnerability reports identifying design flaws that researchers say are enabling variants of the Shai-Hulud supply-chain worm to infect and compromise hundreds of software packages and developer accounts worldwide.

items.title: Fileless Phantom Stealer Targets Browser Credentials
items.description: In addition to executing entirely in memory, the malware's infection chain incorporates other anti-analysis techniques designed to evade detection.

items.title: Security Community Slams US Ban on Exporting Mythos, Fable
items.description: An open letter signed by dozens of security experts asked the government to reverse export restrictions on Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models.

items.title: SprySOCKS Windows Variant Abuses Kernel Drivers to Evade Detection
items.description: FishMonger, a China-nexus threat group, has deployed an undocumented version of the Linux backdoor against government targets in Honduras, Taiwan, Thailand, and Pakistan.

items.title: Rokarolla Android Trojan Levels Up to Full Device Control, Persistence
items.description: The emerging malware, spread via fake TikTok and Chrome downloads, has evolved by combining banking fraud with extensive device surveillance and remote control.

items.title: 'Lorem Ipsum' Malware Pivots to ClickFix Delivery
items.description: New analysis shows the campaign, which uses compromised WordPress sites, may be linked to the ransomware and data extortion group Vice Society.

items.title: From a VHDX File to a Remcos RAT, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: CISA Issues New Directive Improving How Federal Agencies Prioritize the Mitigation of Cyber Vulnerabilities
A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: CISA Announces Winners of the 2026 President’s Cup Cybersecurity Competition
CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
